source.title: A versatile active learning workflow for optimization of genetic and metabolic networks

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Optimization of biological networks is limited by wet lab labor, cost, and lack of convenient computational tools. The goal is to develop a versatile active learning workflow (METIS) that enables data-driven optimization of biological targets with minimal experiments, accessible to experimentalists without programming skills.

section_gists.heading: A versatile active learning workflow for optimization of genetic and metabolic networks
section_gists.gist: Introduces METIS, a modular active machine learning workflow for optimizing biological systems with minimal data. It runs on Google Colab, requires no programming, and is demonstrated on cell-free TXTL, genetic circuits, and the CETCH CO2-fixation cycle. The workflow uses XGBoost after algorithm comparison and aims to democratize machine learning for experimentalists.
process_signals: Problem: optimization limited by labor, cost, and lack of tools.
Hypothesis: active learning can reduce experiments and be made accessible via a simple interface.
Initial algorithm selection: compared DNN, MLP, linear regressors, XGBoost on existing TXTL dataset; XGBoost performed best with small data.
Workflow design: modular, customizable, uses Google Colab for accessibility.
failures_or_limits: DNN and MLP require larger datasets, underperform with limited data.
Need to balance exploration vs exploitation.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Algorithm benchmarking on existing TXTL dataset showed XGBoost and linear regressors outperform DNN/MLP with limited data. XGBoost was chosen for its handling of non-linear interactions and sparsity. Minimum dataset of 10 points per round still allowed optimization. Real-world TXTL optimization validated the workflow. Gene circuit optimization (LacI controller) improved fold-change by two orders of magnitude after identifying resource competition bottleneck via feature importance. CETCH cycle optimization (27 factors, 10^25 combinations) achieved ten-fold productivity improvement with 1000 experiments over 8 rounds, identifying limiting factors (Mco, Hbs, B12).
process_signals: Algorithm comparison: XGBoost > linear regressors > DNN/MLP on small data.
Sample size test: 10 data points per round sufficient.
Real-world TXTL test: workflow validated experimentally.

Gene circuit: initial optimization limited by resource competition; bottleneck identified via feature importance; switching to purified LacI improved fold-change 34-fold without re-running active learning.
CETCH cycle: 1000 experiments over 8 rounds explored 10^25 space; identified limiting factors.
methods: Gold regressor simulation for algorithm comparison
XGBoost with UCB for active learning
Experimental validation in TXTL, gene circuits, CETCH cycle
failures_or_limits: Gene circuit: initial optimization hit bottleneck due to resource competition; required additional experiments with purified LacI.
CETCH cycle: some factors (Mco, Hbs, B12) identified as limiting, indicating incomplete optimization.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: METIS democratizes active learning for experimentalists. Workflow customization depends on combinatorial space and experimental throughput. Exploration-exploitation ratio should decrease over rounds. Can start from scratch or existing data. Feature importance and informative combinations help identify bottlenecks (e.g., LacI circuit). CETCH cycle improved ten-fold with 1000 experiments. Future applications include media optimization, genetic circuits, protein engineering.
process_signals: Validation: METIS successfully optimized diverse systems with minimal experiments.
Limitation: need to balance exploration/exploitation; users should monitor factor fixation.
Bottleneck discovery: feature importance revealed resource competition in LacI circuit; switching to purified LacI bypassed need for re-optimization.
CETCH cycle: identified limiting factors, suggesting further optimization possible.
methods: Active learning with customizable rounds and sample sizes
Feature importance and K most informative combinations for analysis
failures_or_limits: Exploration-exploitation balance must be manually adjusted.
Some bottlenecks require additional experiments outside active learning loop.
Workflow may not fully explore space if rounds/samples insufficient.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Algorithm comparison used a gold regressor trained on 80% of existing TXTL data. Tested MLP, DNN, linear regression, XGBoost with ensembles of 5 models. Active learning core: RandomSearchCV for hyperparameters, ensemble of 20 XGBoost models, UCB scoring for next suggestions. K most informative combinations found by subset training and Spearman correlation. Feature importance from XGBoost built-in. Mutual interactions via linear regression and R-squared.
process_signals: Gold regressor simulation enabled algorithm comparison without wet lab.
Ensemble methods used to improve robustness.
K most informative combinations used for bottleneck analysis.
methods: Ensemble of models (5 for comparison, 20 for active learning)
K most informative combinations via subset training and Spearman correlation
Mutual interaction analysis via linear regression
failures_or_limits: Linear regression is deterministic, so ensemble duplicated.
Increasing subsets for informative combinations increases training time.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Active learning with XGBoost can efficiently optimize biological systems with minimal data.
A modular, user-friendly interface (Google Colab) can democratize machine learning for experimentalists.
Feature importance and informative combinations can reveal bottlenecks and guide hypothesis-driven improvements.
The workflow is versatile across different biological systems (TXTL, genetic circuits, metabolic networks).
uncertainties_to_resolve: How does METIS perform on systems with larger combinatorial spaces or more complex interactions?
Can the exploration-exploitation balance be automated?
What are the limits of XGBoost with extremely sparse data?
How generalizable are the bottleneck discovery methods to other systems?

events.summary: Optimization of biological networks is limited by wet lab labor, cost, and lack of convenient computational tools.
Active learning can reduce experiments and be made accessible via a simple online interface.
Workflow designed to be modular, customizable, and accessible via Google Colab without programming.
Algorithm comparison using gold regressor on existing TXTL dataset; XGBoost and linear regressors outperformed DNN/MLP with limited data.
XGBoost selected for workflow due to handling non-linear interactions and sparsity; minimum dataset of 10 points per round still allowed optimization.
Real-world experimental validation of workflow by optimizing Gfp production in E. coli TXTL system.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction and initial algorithm comparison, establishing problem, hypothesis, design, and early computational validation.

events.summary: Optimized TXTL system composition using active learning over 10 rounds with 20 experiments per round, increasing Gfp yield up to 20-fold.
Feature importance analysis identified tRNA mix and Mg-glutamate as most important components for Gfp yield optimization.
Developed METIS, a modular active learning workflow in Google Colab for easy optimization of biological systems.
Applied METIS to optimize LacI gene circuits, but observed no substantial improvement in fold-change of Gfp production.
Feature importance analysis indicated that the concentration of the P_T7-LacI plasmid was a strong contributor, suggesting resource limitation or deleterious interactions.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers TXTL optimization results, METIS development, and initial LacI circuit optimization with bottleneck identification.

events.summary: Titration experiment showed LacI plasmid has strong negative effect on optimal LacI circuit.
Titration of LacI plasmid with T7 or constitutive promoter against fixed Gfp plasmid showed resource competition.
Results indicated resource competition between two plasmids, with T7 promoter winning competition.
Workflow includes downloading 20 most informative combinations, replacing LacI plasmid with purified LacI, and performing experiment.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains experimental validation and analysis of resource competition bottleneck in LacI circuit optimization, including titration experiments and feature importance.

events.summary: Tested purified LacI protein instead of LacI plasmid to overcome resource competition in TXTL system.
Used METIS module 'K most informative combinations' to extract 20 most informative combinations from active learning cycle.
Repeated 20 setups by replacing P_T7-LacI plasmid with purified LacI protein, resulting in strong improvement in objective function and Gfp fold-change.
All four SLC and MLC circuits improved upon providing external LacI, confirming resource competition as limiting factor.
Continued with one additional round of active learning using the new dataset, improving fold-change to up to 123.
Feature importance module of METIS identified resource competition by LacI plasmid as bottleneck.
Performed 4 rounds of active learning with 50 transcription & translation units tested per round in cell-free system.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk describes experimental validation and application of METIS on LacI gene circuit and transcription & translation unit optimization, including bottleneck identification and improvement steps.

events.summary: Optimized transcription & translation unit with 10,800 potential conditions using only 200 combinations in 4 rounds of active learning.
High exploration rate on Day 3 resulted in wide yield distribution but no further improvement, indicating an optimum was reached.
Investigated 20 most informative combinations in vivo, but cell-free and in vivo yields showed low correlation (0.41).
Continued with one more round of active learning in vivo, improving highest yield by 130%.
CETCH cycle optimization is challenging due to non-linear interactions of enzymes from different organisms, making rational approaches insufficient.
Designed miniaturized CETCH assay with 27 components, using ECHO liquid handler for 125 conditions per round in triplicates.
First five rounds of CETCH optimization used glycolate yield as objective function; after four rounds reached a final...
Switched objective function to efficiency (glycolate yield divided by total enzyme amount) for rounds 6-8.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental results for transcription & translation unit optimization and CETCH cycle optimization, including problem statement, design, experiments, and revision of objective function.

events.summary: Feature importance analysis identified Mco as a top contributor in both optimizations, likely due to its low activity and unstable substrate.
During efficiency optimization, Hbs and coenzyme B12 were the most important components; high Hbs decreased yield by 40%, while low Hbs did not lower yield.
Users must manually adjust exploration-exploitation ratio per round to avoid premature factor fixation or random combinations.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers CETCH cycle optimization results, efficiency optimization, bottleneck analysis, validation, and discussion of workflow limitations.

events.summary: Design of mutual interaction analysis algorithm using linear regression and R squared to detect epistatic effects.
Design of METIS prediction mode to maximize model performance by selecting most uncertain combinations.
Validation of model performance using k-fold cross-validation for enzyme engineering notebook.
Design of table-to-speech virtual assistant to aid manual liquid handling by reading volumes and destinations.
Experimental protocol for cell-free reactions using table-to-speech assistant to minimize pipetting errors.
RT-qPCR experiment to quantify mRNA levels, with additional DNase treatment to remove plasmid DNA contamination.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily describes detailed experimental methods and computational algorithms for mutual interaction analysis, METIS prediction mode, cross-validation, and lab automation tools. It contains design and experimental events but no direct problem statements or major findings.

events.summary: LC-MS analysis of CoA esters using triple quadrupole mass spectrometer with C18 column and external standard curves.
Data analysis performed using Microsoft Excel, GraphPad Prism, custom Python scripts, and Agilent Mass Hunter software.
Data and code availability: active learning data and Google Colab notebooks provided on GitHub for reproducibility.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily contains detailed experimental methods (LC-MS protocols, reaction conditions) and data/code availability statements. It does not describe research evolution events like problem, hypothesis, design, or revision. Only a few events related to experimental procedures and data analysis are extracted.
This chunk contains only author contributions, funding, competing interests, and license information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: Optimization of biological networks is often limited by wet lab labor and cost, and the lack of convenient computational tools.
Here, we describe METIS, a versatile active machine learning workflow with a simple online interface for the data-driven optimization of biological targets with minimal experiments.

We created METIS for experimentalists with no experience in programming, who can use the entire process of personalized active learning, experimental setup, data analysis and visualization without any advanced computational skills.
We used the gold regressor to assess the performance of four different machine learning algorithms over 10 rounds of active learning (Fig. 1a).
XGBoost outperformed linear regressors when fewer data points per round were used (Supplementary Fig. 1).

Having validated the workflow with an existing data set, we next sought to test it in a real-world experimental setup, simulating a situation in which the number of combinations that can be tested is limited by available equipment, readout and experimental cost.
We chose (again) to optimize relative Gfp production (yield) in an E. coli
performed an active learning process over 10 rounds with only 20 experiments per round
the relative yield increased up to 20
tRNA mix and Mg-glutamate were the most important components in optimizing Gfp yield, while cAMP and NAD were the least important contributors
we sought to build METIS, a modular architecture that can be easily applied for the optimization of different biological objective functions
implemented our workflow in Google Colab Python notebooks that can be accessed by the user—without installation or registration
we did not observe a substantial improvement in fold-change of Gfp production alone
Feature importance analysis identified the concentration of the $P_{T7}-LacI$ plasmid as strong contributor
indicating deleterious LacI protein-DNA interactions or resource limitation of the TXTL system through production of the lacI protein
By performing a titration experiment with $P_{T7}-LacI$ , we could show that addition of the LacI plasmid has indeed a strong negative effect on the optimal LacI circuit (i.e., with pTHS)
we titrated the LacI plasmid with either T7 or a constitutive promoter against a fixed concentration of the Gfp expressing plasmid under control of either T7 or a constitutive promoter
These results indicated a resource competition between the two plasmids, according to which the T7 promoter wins competition
Downloaded 20 most informative combinations
Replaced LacI plasmid with purified LacI
Performed the experiment, input to the model as Day_0
To overcome resource competition, we tested purified LacI protein instead of the LacI plasmid in the TXTL system, which resulted in improved Gfp productivity
Using a module of METIS called “K most informative combinations” (with the number K to be defined by the user), we extracted the 20 most informative combinations of the active learning cycle
repeated these 20 setup by replacing $P_{T7}-LacI$ plasmid with purified LacI protein (Fig. 3j), resulting in a strong improvement in the objective function, and in particular Gfp fold-change

Note that among these 20 combinations were again all four SLC and MLC circuits. All of them improved upon providing external LacI, clearly demonstrating that resource competition had been limiting performance of the SLC and MLC circuits.

Continuing with only one additional round of active learning using this dataset, we were able to improve the fold-change to up to 123 (Fig. 3k), which is 15-fold improvement compared to that of 10 rounds of active learning with the $P_{T7}-LacI$ plasmid and 34-fold improvement compared to the initial setup.

the feature importance module of METIS, which identified apparent bottlenecks (i.e., resource competition by the LacI plasmid) and the K most informative combinations module of the workflow were crucial for success
The plot representing the average of triplicates (n = 3 independent experiments) as the result of 4 rounds of active learning, with 50 transcription & translation units tested per round.
Over 4 rounds of active learning, yield of the transcription & translation unit improved up to 12-fold on Day 3.
Using a high exploration rate on Day 3 resulted in a wide distribution of yields, but no further improvement, indicating that an optimum had been reached
we additionally investigated the effect of the 20 most informative combinations in vivo
the cell-free and in vivo yields for the 20 combinations showed a relatively low correlation of 0.41
we used the data from Day 0 and continued with one more round of experiments guided by our workflow (Day 1, Fig. 4f). This resulted in an improvement by 130% for the highest yield in vivo.

since the enzymes used for its construction derive from different organisms and thus metabolic backgrounds, several rounds of rational optimization were needed to harmonize the enzyme reactions and cofactors used in the cycle; and even though the kinetic parameters of the individual enzymes are known, their interactions in such a complex setup are non-linear, hardly predictable and basically impossible to disentangle with pure rational approaches.
The setup of the CETCH cycle consists of 27 components encompassing 13 core enzymes, as well as four accessory enzymes, and nine other components
For the first five rounds of optimization, we used product yield (glycolate) as objective function
After four iterative rounds, we reached a fina
the data of three additional rounds of optimization with efficiency as the objective function (rounds 6-8)
One of the most important contributors for both optimization efforts is the enzyme Methylsuccinyl-CoA dehydrogenase (Mco)
The enzyme's low activity of 0.1 U/mg and its unstable substrate methylsuccinyl-CoA, which is prone to spontaneous hydrolysis, likely require large amounts of Mco to preserve flux through the cycle
increasing Hbs concentration in the original assay decreased yield by 40%, while decreasing Hbs by one order of magnitude did not lower glycolate yield

Users should apply their knowledge on the system and implicitly check whether the value of a given factor is fixed too early, probably indicating a low exploration to exploitation ratio. On the other hand, a high exploration to exploitation ratio might push the model towards random combinations, asking for a proper balance
The mutual interactions were calculated through the following algorithm:
A linear regression model is fitted on the dataset and its performance is evaluated based on the R squared of predicted and actual values.
The difference between each performance and the baseline, j, is calculated and visualized.
METIS prediction aims to maximize the model performance on the prediction of the objective function for unseen combinations.
At the end of each round, it returns a trained model instead of promising combinations, and the R squared of prediction is improved over rounds.
To evaluate the model performance of the enzyme engineering notebook, we used k-fold cross-validation.
In the end, the average performance on all subsets was reported as the model's performance.

This tool helps molecular biologists to boost their manual liquid handling through reading volume and destination well in ascending order, therefore minimizes the need for changing the pipetting volume.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Optimization of biological networks is limited by wet lab labor, cost, and lack of convenient computational tools.

canonical_events.summary: Optimization of biological networks is limited by wet lab labor, cost, and lack of convenient computational tools.
Active learning can reduce experiments and be made accessible via a simple online interface.
Workflow designed to be modular, customizable, and accessible via Google Colab without programming.
Algorithm comparison using gold regressor on existing TXTL dataset; XGBoost and linear regressors outperformed DNN/MLP with limited data.
XGBoost selected for workflow due to handling non-linear interactions and sparsity; minimum dataset of 10 points per round still allowed optimization.
Real-world experimental validation of workflow by optimizing Gfp production in E. coli TXTL system over 10 rounds with 20 experiments per round, increasing yield up to 20-fold.
Feature importance analysis identified tRNA mix and Mg-glutamate as most important components for Gfp yield optimization.
Applied METIS to optimize LacI gene circuits, but observed no substantial improvement in fold-change of Gfp production.
Feature importance analysis indicated that the concentration of the P_T7-LacI plasmid was a strong contributor, suggesting resource limitation or deleterious interactions.
Titration experiment showed LacI plasmid has strong negative effect on optimal LacI circuit, indicating resource competition between plasmids.
Workflow includes downloading 20 most informative combinations, replacing LacI plasmid with purified LacI, and performing experiment.
Repeated 20 setups by replacing P_T7-LacI plasmid with purified LacI protein, resulting in strong improvement in objective function and Gfp fold-change.
All four SLC and MLC circuits improved upon providing external LacI, confirming resource competition as limiting factor.
Continued with one additional round of active learning using the new dataset, improving fold-change to up to 123 (15-fold improvement over 10 rounds with plasmid, 34-fold over initial).
Feature importance module of METIS identified resource competition by LacI plasmid as bottleneck.
Optimized transcription & translation unit with 10,800 potential conditions using only 200 combinations in 4 rounds of active learning, achieving 12-fold improvement.
High exploration rate on Day 3 resulted in wide yield distribution but no further improvement, indicating an optimum was reached.
Investigated 20 most informative combinations in vivo, but cell-free and in vivo yields showed low correlation (0.41).

Hypothesis leads to design of accessible workflow.
Design requires algorithm selection via comparison.
Comparison results lead to selection of XGBoost.
Selected algorithm used in real-world TXTL validation.

Workflow applied to next system: LacI gene circuits.
Feature importance analysis after circuit optimization attempt.
Feature importance suggests resource competition, tested via titration.
Titration confirms resource competition, leading to design of purified LacI approach.
Design executed: replace plasmid with purified LacI.
Improvement validates that resource competition was limiting.
Validation enables further active learning round for fold-change improvement.
Feature importance analysis confirms bottleneck identification.
Workflow applied to transcription & translation unit optimization.
Analysis of exploration rate effect after optimization.
In vivo testing of informative combinations from cell-free optimization.

must_cover_points: Problem of optimization limited by labor/cost/tools.
Hypothesis of active learning with accessible interface.
Design of modular, no-code Google Colab workflow.
Algorithm comparison and selection of XGBoost.
Real-world TXTL validation with 20-fold yield improvement.
LacI circuit optimization failure due to resource competition.
Feature importance identifies LacI plasmid as bottleneck.
Switch to purified LacI and 20 informative combinations.
Validation that resource competition was limiting.
Additional active learning round achieves 34-fold improvement.
